hook: You already know how to plot points and work with vectors in a flat, two-dimensional plane — but the real world has depth. This unit breaks through that flat surface and extends everything you know into full three-dimensional space, equipping you with tools used in physics, engineering, computer graphics, and robotics. What does it even mean to find the equation of a plane, or to describe a curved surface algebraically?
concept: The unifying idea is that the algebraic tools of coordinates, vectors, and equations can be extended into 3D to precisely describe the geometry of points, lines, planes, and surfaces — a framework known as analytic geometry in three dimensions.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to locate points in 3D space using $(x, y, z)$ coordinates, calculate distances between points, and find midpoints using the natural extensions of familiar 2D formulas.
Master representing, adding, and scaling 3D vectors using component form, and apply the dot product to find angles and determine when vectors are orthogonal.
Apply the cross product to find a vector perpendicular to two given vectors in space, and use it to compute areas of parallelograms and solve geometric problems unique to 3D.
You will learn to write parametric and symmetric equations for lines and derive scalar equations for planes using direction vectors, normal vectors, and points in 3D space.
Recognize and sketch classic 3D surfaces — including spheres, cylinders, and quadric surfaces — by analyzing their equations and understanding how cross-sections reveal their shape.
